title: PHP-FPM Memory per Worker Trend from Status and Host Metrics
dataSources: `index=web` `sourcetype=phpfpm:status` (`active_processes`, `total_processes`) joined with `index=os` process or host memory metrics (`ps`, Perfmon, or `Splunk_TA_nix`)
app: Splunk Universal Forwarder; optional Splunk Add-on for Unix and Linux (`Splunk_TA_nix`); custom `props.conf`/`transforms.conf` for `phpfpm:*` sourcetypes
spl:
index=web sourcetype="phpfpm:status"
| eval ap=tonumber(active_processes), tp=tonumber(total_processes)
| join type=left host [
    search index=os sourcetype=ps earliest=-2m
    | stats latest(mem_used) as rss_kb by host
]
| eval approx_mb_per_worker=if(tp>0, round(rss_kb/1024/tp,2), null())
| timechart span=15m avg(approx_mb_per_worker) by pool
dataModelAcceleration: Not applicable: this use case is not built on a CIM data model. Rely on the search in Step 2 against your raw logs or metrics.
description: Rightsizing `pm.max_children` needs approximate RSS per PHP worker. Comparing FPM process counts with host or cgroup memory avoids OOM kills during traffic spikes while limiting over-provisioning.
value: Reduces user-facing latency and 5xx rates by exposing pool saturation, slow scripts, and worker stability before front proxies fail.
grandmaExplanation: We use this to reduce user-facing latency and 5xx rates by exposing pool saturation, slow scripts, and worker stability before front proxies fail.
implementation: Ingest `ps` or `top` snapshot via TA-nix; alias mem field. For container hosts, use cAdvisor/kube stats joined on pod/host.
visualization: Time series (pool utilization, queue, latency percentiles), single value alerts, top charts for scripts and methods.
knownFalsePositives: Thread or worker pools fill during traffic spikes, slow upstream services, or DoS-style load. A surge alone can be benign if backends recover.
